body_txt: A situation happens where the hero is forced into an unfamiliar vehicle, either Falling into the Cockpit or hijacking the first thing they could find . Somehow, they get it to work with little trouble , though maybe started in reverse before correcting . It doesn't matter if it's a tiny Volkswagen Beetle, an 800-horsepower Lamborghini with a six-speed manual transmission, an 18-wheel truck with a double clutch, or the biggest earth-moving machine on a mining site. As long as the keys are in it, it's fair game for a joyride or hasty escape. Heck, even if there are no keys, a Hassle-Free Hotwire is a minor inconvenience. In a modern society, most anyone with a driver's license can be expected to get a car or light truck started and moving in some direction, since the controls are more or less standardized, but beyond that realm, most professional or commercial vehicles require some specialized training or familiarity with their controls to drive. Even a motorcycle is beyond the experience of many people. What's that lever on the left handlebar? If you said 'brake' guess again, that thing is your clutch. Things like bulldozers, tanks, tractor-trailers, ships, railroad locomotives, etc. would probably leave an inexperienced person at a loss to use if you dropped them behind the controls in an emergency situation and told them to "get us out of here!" Yet time after time, characters just take off as if they operated one for a living for a decade. Sometimes the character fails badly and wrecks the vehicle seconds later, protesting "I told you I don't know how to drive one of these!" or else you get an I Know Mortal Kombat , Suddenly Always Knew That or You Didn't Ask answer as to why they didn't crash. This can sometimes be justified through the training and experience of the character. An Ace Pilot might be familiar with fighter jets but will take a few seconds to figure out a helicopter. A group may even get an alien spaceship and turn to their one pilot friend as the only one who may be able to figure it out. Though aircraft in general are usually exempt from this. The plea of "Can anyone here fly a plane?!" when the original pilots get incapacitated is basically a trope by itself . In video games it is usually Acceptable Breaks from Reality , as the control interface of a keyboard, joystick or controller will never quite be the same thing, and learning curves are expected to be fairly intuitive to start off. That said, elaborate training simulators can often get very realistic or at least offer a full manual with instructions and an owner's manual so you can identify what all the controls mean, though is never quite the same thing as getting behind a high-performance race car. Holders of this license are usually also qualified as a Crew of One . Compare Improbable Piloting Skills , Damn You, Muscle Memory! and Only I Can Make It Go . Contrast Driving Stick .
